title: The Loading Waterfall
intro: A user reports that your website takes 8 seconds to load. Think of it like a group trip where everyone is waiting on someone else before they can leave -- each file waits for another to finish. You open the browser DevTools and see a waterfall chart showing exactly what's happening during those 8 seconds. Let's investigate.

teach.title: Loading = Assembly Line
teach.body: When a browser loads a page, it's like an assembly line. First it downloads the HTML, then it discovers CSS and JavaScript files it needs, then it downloads those, then it finds images referenced in the CSS, and so on. Each step can't start until the previous one finishes. This chain of dependent downloads is called a **waterfall**.

teach.title: Waterfall = Waiting for Friends to Get Ready
teach.body: You've planned a group trip. But Sarah can't leave until Tom picks her up, Tom can't leave until he gets the address from you, and you can't send the address until you confirm the restaurant. Everyone is waiting on someone else. A **loading waterfall** is the same -- each file waits for another to finish before it can even start. Breaking these unnecessary chains lets everyone get ready at the same time.

teach.title: What a Waterfall Shows
teach.body:
A waterfall chart in DevTools shows every file your page requests, lined up on a timeline. You can see:

1. **What** was downloaded (HTML, CSS, JS, images, fonts)
2. **When** each download started and finished
3. **How long** each request took
4. **What blocked what** -- which files had to wait for others

Long bars and late-starting requests are your clues to what's slow.

teach.title: A Typical Waterfall Problem
teach.annotation: Each script blocks the next one from loading. The browser downloads them one by one instead of in parallel. Moving scripts to the bottom of the body or adding 'async'/'defer' fixes this.

teach.body: Knowing how to read a waterfall chart means you can spot the exact bottleneck that's making users bail. Fix one chained dependency and you might cut load time in half -- keeping users engaged and paying.
The loading waterfall shows every file your page downloads and what's blocking what. Most slow pages have a long chain of dependent requests that could be loaded in parallel instead.

start.narrative:
A user reports your landing page takes 8 seconds to load. You open Chrome DevTools Network tab and see the waterfall:

1. index.html (200ms)
2. styles.css (300ms) -- starts after HTML
3. analytics.js (500ms) -- starts after HTML
4. app.js (800ms) -- waits for analytics.js to finish
5. hero-image.png (2.1s) -- waits for CSS to finish
6. Font files (1.2s) -- waits for CSS to finish
7. API call for content (1.8s) -- waits for app.js to finish

What's the biggest problem here?

choices.text: app.js is waiting for analytics.js to finish before it can start loading
The hero image is too large at 2.1 seconds
There are too many files being loaded

found-blocking.narrative: Exactly. analytics.js is render-blocking, app.js waits for it, then the API call waits for app.js: a 3.1-second sequential chain. How do you unblock app.js?

choices.text: Add 'async' to analytics.js so it loads without blocking other scripts
Combine all JavaScript into one giant bundle

image-issue.narrative: The image is heavy but loads in parallel with fonts -- it's not blocking other resources. The real cost is the JS chain: analytics → app.js → API. Three seconds of sequential waiting that could be parallel.

choices.text: Focus on the JS blocking chain instead

too-many-files.narrative: 7 files isn't unusual. The problem isn't the count -- it's the dependencies. Browsers can download 6+ files in parallel when nothing blocks them.

choices.text: Look at what's blocking parallel downloads

fix-resources.narrative: Scripts are unblocked. But the hero image and fonts both wait for styles.css to finish before they can even start downloading. Users see a blank page until CSS arrives. How do you start the critical resources sooner?

choices.text: Add <link rel="preload"> for the hero image and fonts in the HTML head
Inline the font @font-face rules in the HTML head so they discover sooner
Move the <link rel="stylesheet"> to the bottom of the body

fix-api-chain.narrative: Critical assets now start downloading in the first round-trip. But the API call for page content still waits for app.js to download AND parse before it can fire -- and the API itself takes 1.8s. How do you cut that chain?

choices.text: Server-render the initial content so the page is meaningful before any JS runs
Add a <link rel="preload"> hint for the API endpoint so the browser starts the fetch in parallel
Fire the API call from a tiny inline <script> in the head, before app.js loads

measure-and-monitor.narrative: Local DevTools says the page is fast now. But your user is on a 4G phone in another country. How do you confirm the fix actually helps real users -- and how do you catch a regression next time someone adds a script?

choices.text: Run Lighthouse + WebPageTest from a real device profile, then enable Real User Monitoring (RUM) in production
Run Lighthouse once on your laptop and call it done
Skip measurement -- the waterfall looks fine in DevTools

end-good.narrative: You unblocked the JS chain with async, preloaded the hero and fonts so they didn't wait for CSS, killed the API-after-JS chain with server rendering, and validated the result with Lighthouse, WebPageTest, and ongoing RUM. Total load drops from 8s to under 2s for real users, and your monitoring will alert you the next time someone adds a render-blocking script.
end-good.feedback: Complete performance loop: identify the bottleneck, unblock JS, prioritize critical resources, eliminate the API chain, then measure on real conditions and monitor in production. Each step removed a different kind of waterfall dependency.

end-okay.narrative: Most chains are fixed and the waterfall looks much better. But one Lighthouse run on a fast laptop misses what real mobile users actually experience, and there's no monitoring -- the next render-blocking script will silently re-introduce the 8-second load.
end-okay.feedback: Solid technical fixes; weak verification loop. Performance regressions are constant. Without RUM and a budget in CI, the gains erode within months.

end-poor.narrative: The technical fixes shipped, but you never confirmed the result on real conditions. You're running on a fast laptop with a fast network. Mobile users on 4G might still be staring at a blank page for 5 seconds.
end-poor.feedback: DevTools timing on your machine is the easiest case in the world. Always validate against a throttled network and a real device profile, then set up RUM so the next regression doesn't slip past you.
